coredata.dc:title: Tunable dual fluorescence emissions with high photoluminescence quantum yields modulated by Na ion dispersion method for purely solid state N-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Kaiyue
Shi, Yanan
Jia, Yan
Li, Peng
Zhang, Xiaotao
Feng, Xia
Zhu, Lina
Sun, Yan
Hu, Wenping
Zhao, Guangjiu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we reported a facile Na ion dispersion (NID) method for simply preparing a series of purely solid state N-doped carbon dots (NCDots) with tunable photoluminescence from blue to yellow light. We demonstrated for the first time that tunable multicolor photoluminescence is ascribed as dual fluorescence emissions modulated by the ratio between surface state (SS) and core state (CS) of NCDots, respectively. Moreover, we revealed that the emission of CS is determined by heterocycle N and the emission of SS is related to amino N and Na ion. Furthermore, the common aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon of solid state CDots is efficiently inhibited by introducing NID effect. Therefore, photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of our purely solid state NCDots could be up to 80.7 %, to the best of our knowledge, which is the highest PLQY for the purely solid state CDots until now. This work paves the way for the development of purely solid state CDots with high PLQY and tunable multicolor photoluminescence and applications in optoelectronic device and cell imaging fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid state
PLQY
Dual fluorescence emissions
Cell imaging

UV–vis absorption spectra and emission spectra

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Solid state N-doped carbon dots synthesized from trisodium citrate dihydrate and urea by microwave method. The core structure of the NCDots is composed of heterocyclic N and the surface structure is consisting of amino, carboxyl and Na ion.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of all N-doped carbon dots in solid state. (b-h) UV–vis absorption spectra (green solid area) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (hollow solid line) with different excitation wavelength of all solid state N-doped carbon dots (0.5U, 1.0U, 1.5U, 2.0U, 2.5U, 3.0U, 3.5U).
Mechanism representation of the dual fluorescence emission of solid state N-doped carbon dots.
(a) TEM image (b) size distribution of TEM (c) FTIR (d-f) C1s N1s and O1s XPS of 3.0U solid state N-doped carbon dots.

(a–f) CLSM images of BEL-7402 cells incubated with 500 μg/mL of the 0.5U, 1.5U and 3.0U solid state N-doped carbon dots respectively. (d-f) bright field, and (a-c) the excitation wavelength is 405 nm. The scale bar represents 30 μm.

head.ce:title: Tunable dual fluorescence emissions with high photoluminescence quantum yields modulated by Na ion dispersion method for purely solid state N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Molecular Sciences and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao, 266235, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Molecular Sciences and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Molecular Sciences and Engineering, Shandong University, Qingdao 266235, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported a facile Na ion dispersion (NID) method for simply preparing a series of purely solid state N-doped carbon dots (NCDots) with tunable photoluminescence from blue to yellow light. We demonstrated for the first time that tunable multicolor photoluminescence is ascribed as dual fluorescence emissions modulated by the ratio between surface state (SS) and core state (CS) of NCDots, respectively. Moreover, we revealed that the emission of CS is determined by heterocycle N and the emission of SS is related to amino N and Na ion. Furthermore, the common aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon of solid state CDots is efficiently inhibited by introducing NID effect. Therefore, photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of our purely solid state NCDots could be up to 80.7 %, to the best of our knowledge, which is the highest PLQY for the purely solid state CDots until now. This work paves the way for the development of purely solid state CDots with high PLQY and tunable multicolor photoluminescence and applications in optoelectronic device and cell imaging fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As a class of emerging luminescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDots) have received extensive attention owing to their special merits (such as good stability, low toxicity, easy preparation, excellent biocompatibility) and have been successfully employed in various applications in biomedical imaging, photocatalytic, data encryption and optoelectronic devices [1–15]. However, on account of excessive resonance energy transfer [16] or direct π-π interactions [17], CDots always suffer aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) in solid state [18–20]. As a consequence, only few synthesized solid state CDots are photoluminescent [21–25]. This photoluminescence quenching phenomenon restricts the application of solid state CDots greatly.

Recently, lots of researchers devoted to prevent ACQ for CDots in the solid state. One method is to embed CDots in water-like matrices such as starch, sodium chloride, barium sulfate, polyvinyl alcohol, polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxanes and silica [26–34]. By adding these matrices, non-radiative pathways including excessive resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interactions are destroyed, resulting these solid state CDots emit fluorescence through radiation channal. Additionally, adding polymer steric hindrance chains to the surface of CDots has been proven an efficient method to conquer ACQ effect [35]. However, although these methods could prevent ACQ effect, most of the reported PLQYs are still not high enough. For instance, Qu and coworkers prepared the solid state CDots through a CDots-triggered silica gelation process, which PLQY could only reach 52 % [36]. In addition, they obtained the 30–40 % PLQY of solid state CDots by incorporating CDots into the silica network [37]. Yang and coworkers synthesized polymer solid state CDots which PLQY only could be up to 8.50 % [38]. More importantly, these methods only conquer the ACQ effect in non-luminous synthesized solid state CDots and did not solve the ACQ effect by themselves. Therefore, it is of great importance to directly develop higher PLQY of purely solid state CDots without any further treatments.

According to these considerations, we have developed a new Na ion dispersion (NID) method for preparing purely solid state NCDots to satisfy the urgent need to improve PLQY for the solid state CDots. The sodium ion could attach to the defects at the surface of CDots and keep the CDots at a suitable distance to achieve the effect of dispersing CDots apart. This method could prevent ACQ effectively. Therefore, we performed NID method to directly prepare NCDots by using trisodium citrate dihydrate and urea, as shown in Scheme 1 . The prepared solid powders of our NCDots are shown in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical reagents and preparation of solid state NCDots
ce:section.ce:para: Trisodium citrate dihydrate was purchased from Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Urea was purchased from Heowns Biochem LLC (Tianjin, China). All the initial chemicals in this work were used without further purification. The water used in all experiments was deionized water.

Solid state NCDots were prepared through microwave method. 3.0 g trisodium citrate dihydrate and 0.5 g–3.5 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. In order to enhance the homogeneity of the mixture solution, the obtained solution was treated with ultrasonic. Next, the mixture solution was placed in the domestic microwave oven heating by middle high fire (750 W) in approximately 5 min. Eventually, the mixture solution changed from being a colorless liquid to clustered solid, which indicates the formation of solid state NCDots. All the solid state NCDots were prepared with the same conditions. The solid state NCDots of 0.5U, 1.0U, 1.5U, 2.0U, 2.5U, 3.0U, 3.5U were obtained corresponding to the urea content of 0.5 g, 1.0 g, 1.5 g, 2.0 g, 2.5 g, 3.0 g, 3.5 g, respectively.

The morphology and structure of solid state NCDots were analyzed by field-transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30) operating. The specific operation details are dissolving the NCDots powder in ethanol. After ultrasonic treatment, using a dropper to take the solution and dropping it on a copper mesh with a supporting film. After drying, it can be observed by electron microscope. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB MK2 with Al Kα radiation (hν = 1,486.6 eV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed with a Bruker Advance D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was conducted on ALPHA FT-IR spectrophotometer from 370 to 7500 cm−1 using infrared reflection method. UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed through absorption scan in two integrating sphere on a Shimadzu UV-3600 PLUS Spectrofluorometer at range from 185 to 3300 nm. NCDots were added an integrating sphere and BaSO4 power as a referenced sample was added another integrating sphere, diffuse reflection is the measuring principle of the Spectrofluorometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of NCDots in solid state were obtained using F-380 fluorescence Spectrofluorometer with emission scan in an integrating sphere.

The PLQY were acquired using an Edinburgh-steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS1000 system equipped with a xenon lamp, calibrated integrating sphere, and the Fluorescence Analysis Software Technology (FAST) package provides unsurpassed accuracy. The PLQY in solid state was calculated by the equation: ηQY = (IS-IR)/(ER-ES), in which IS represents the luminescence emission spectrum of the NCDots sample, IR is the luminescence emission spectrum from the reference (BaSO4 powders), ER is the excitation spectrum for exciting the reference, and ES is the excitation spectrum for exciting the sample. The calculation method of PLQY in aqueous solution is the same as above, except that the reference is replaced by water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption spectra and emission spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of all the solid state NCDots (0.5U, 1.0U, 1.5U, 2.0U, 2.5U, 3.0U, 3.5U) are shown in Fig. 1 a. Typical π-π* transition in the region of 200−300 nm is attributed to the CC bonds [39], while the absorption peak at 300−400 nm is originated from n-π* transition of CO and CN groups [40]. Moreover, the visible region (400–700 nm) is contributed to the absorption of amino N and sodium ion. The absorption spectra shift to the red gradually with the increase of the nitrogen content following by the enhancement of the second peak from 300 to 700 nm simultaneously.

Then the emission spectra of all solid state NCDots were obtained in different excitation wavelength, which are shown in Fig. 1b–h. Firstly, we compared the emission spectra of different solid state NCDots at the same excitation wavelength. With the nitrogen content increases, the emission peak at 410 nm decreases gradually and accompanied by an increase in 500 nm. Furthermore, we compared the emission spectra of the same solid state NCDots at different excitation wavelengths. When excitation wavelength is increased from 300 to 440 nm, the emission peak has almost no spectra shift. The excitation-independent luminescence feature mainly owes to the uniform size and surface state of NCDots [41,42]. With further increasing the excitation wavelength from 440 to 500 nm, the emission peak shifts to longer wavelength. This change might be attributed to the reabsorption effect resulting from the partial overlap between absorption and emission spectra [43,44]. Moreover, we observed that the NCDots exhibit a single emission peak at 500 nm in the low energy excitation region above 400 nm and present a dual fluorescence emission character at 410 nm and 500 nm in the high energy excitation region below 400 nm.

It is known that the large conjugated aromatic rings such as pyrrolic and pyridinic ring composed of CC and CN groups belong to the core and the amino N and sodium ion of structural units belong to the surface of solid state NCDots [45,46]. At the same time, the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N are ascribed to the absorption spectra at 200−400 nm region. In addition, amino N and sodium ion contribute to the visible absorption spectra from 400 to 700 nm. Therefore, the high energy excitation region below 400 nm should be attributed to the core state (CS) and the low energy excitation region above 400 nm is derived from the surface state (SS) of NCDots. As a consequence, it has been demonstrated that the emission peak at 410 nm is mainly attributed to the CS state, while the emission peak at 500 nm is attributed to both the CS and SS states.

According to the spectra properties of solid state NCDots, our proposed mechanism diagram of the dual fluorescence emissions of purely solid state NCDots is presented in Fig. 2 . Solid state NCDots were excited to CS below 400 nm excitation. After the activated excitons located in CS undergo a process of recombination of electron and holes, there are mainly two pathways to decay to the ground state. One pathway is to directly emit 410 nm photoluminescence through the radiation channel. The other pathway is first relaxing from CS to SS state and then to emit 500 nm photoluminescence. On the other hand, when the excitation wavelength is above 400 nm, the solid state NCDots would be photoexcited to the SS state directly. Therefore, the photoluminescence at 410 nm will be impossible and only the photoluminescence at 500 nm from the SS state is feasible. It is worth noting that the amino N and Na ion of SS could generate surface defects. By adjusting the ratio of N and Na, different trap states can be generated. The 500 nm photoluminescence could shift to 560 nm with the dependence of excitation wavelength in our work. Therefore, by regulating the ratio between SS and CS of NCDots, the dual fluorescence emission could be modulated and the tunable multicolor photoluminescence from blue to yellow could be realized.

The photoluminescence quantum yields of these solid state NCDots were tested and presented in Table S1. By regulating the ratio of nitrogen and sodium reasonably, we obtained purely powdery solid state CDots with high PLQY which can be up to 80.7 % for the 3.0U solid state NCDots. As far as we know, this is the first time to obtain such high PLQY from synthesized purely solid state CDots. At the same time, the high PLQY demonstrates that our NID method can effectively prevent ACQ. In detail, by introducing Na ion into the raw material for preparing solid state NCDots, the formed Na ion could attach to the surface of NCDots and further keep the NCDots at a suitable distance. This distance could prevent the NCDots from accumulating together, making them spread and dispersed evenly. Eventually, solid state NCDots could emit light by overcoming the aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect. Moreover, too little sodium does not effectively disperse the NCDots apart and too much sodium will lead to salinization and accumulation of NCDots. As a consequence, by introducing Na ion and regulating the nitrogen and sodium at a suitable ratio will lead to such a high PLQY.

To figure out the feature of the CDots, structural characterization of all solid state NCDots were carried out, which are presented in supporting information. The 3.0U solid state NCDots were selected to illustrate the structural character. The TEM pattern is shown in Fig. 3 a, the pattern reveals the nanoparticles are separated well with each other. The corresponding nanoparticles size distribution histogram is obtained by measuring 80 particles, as indicated in Fig. 3b. The size of solid state NCDots taken on Gaussian distribute between 2.0 and 6.0 nm with an average diameter about 3.8 nm. The X-ray diffraction is shown in Fig. S2. The pattern exhibit a broad peak at 23.5° and a minor sharp peak at 31°, both of which are attributed to the graphitic structure. The broad peak at 23.5° is corresponding to the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of graphite and the minor sharp peak is attributed to disordered surface of solid state NCDots [47]. This proves large conjugated core structure and amorphous surface structure of the solid state NCDots.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum is shown in Fig. 3c, this spectra demonstrates that there are a large amount of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups in solid state NCDots. The strong and broad peak at 3500−3400 cm−1 is corresponding to the OH and NH stretching vibrations [48].The middle strong peak at around 2260−2170 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CN group. The peak at 1630−1390 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO or CN group [49]. In addition, absorption bands located at 630 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CH group. As shown in Fig. S3, all of these solid state NCDots have same transmittance peaks, the presence of these hydrophilic groups demonstrates that all of these purely solid state CDots have good water solubility [50].

To investigate the composition and valance state of solid state NCDots, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed, as presented in Fig. 3d–f. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum indicates five characteristic peaks of CO/CN, CO, CN, CC and CC at 288.9, 288.0, 286.5, 285.0 and 284.5 eV, respectively [51,52]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum reveals the existence of three different types of nitrogen: pyrrolic N at 400.0 eV, -NH2 at 399.5 eV and pyridinic N at 398.3 eV, respectively [53]. The O1s XPS spectrum shows three peaks at 535.7, 532.0 and 531.1 eV corresponding to the COOH/H2O, CO and CO respectively [54]. The full scan XPS spectrum, as shown in Fig. S4, presents that all of the solid state NCDots predominately consist of C and O elements with minor N and Na elements [55]. The characteristic peaks also confirm the presence of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N at the core and amino N, Na ion at the surface of our solid state NCDots. Furthermore, the element percentage of all the solid state NCDots was calculated according to the XPS spectrum. Nitrogen content increases gradually with the ratio of urea rising, while the sodium element is showing a decreasing trend roughly, as shown in Table S2. By adjusting the nitrogen and sodium contents at a suitable ratio, we finally increased the PLQY to 80.7 %.

On the basics of the outstanding luminescent character of our solid state NCDots, we chose BEL-7402 cell to investigate the cellular imaging ability of 0.5U, 1.5U and 3.0U solid state NCDots respectively, which are shown in Fig. 4 . The 500 ug/mL solid state NCDots were incubated with the BEL-7402 cell for 10 min, followed by observation under a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). It is clear that the solid state NCDots gradually accumulate in the organelle and the blue, green and yellow PL are observed under 405 nm light excitation. The yellow PL corresponding to 3.0U solid state NCDots is not clear enough, which is probably due to the solvation effect of NCDots in water solvent. The above results confidently demonstrate the potential bioimaging application of our NCDots with satisfactory PLQY.

In summary, we first proposed a facile NID method for directly preparing a series of purely solid state NCDots with tunable multicolor photoluminescence from blue to yellow. It was demonstrated that the tunable multicolor photoluminescence is ascribed as the dual fluorescence emission modulated by the ratio between SS and CS of NCDots, respectively. We revealed that the emission of CS is determined by heterocycle N and the emission of SS is related to amino N and Na ion. Furthermore, the common ACQ phenomenon of solid state CDots is efficiently inhibited by introducing NID effect. Therefore, the PLQY of our purely solid state NCDots could be up to 80.7 %, to the best of our knowledge, which is the highest PLQY of purely solid state CDots until now. This work paves the way for the development of purely solid state CDots with high PLQY and tunable multicolor photoluminescence and applications in optoelectronic device and cell imaging fields.

Zhao design and managed the project. K. Zhang, Y. Shi, Y. Jia, L. Zhu, and Y. Sun performed the measurements. K. Zhang, Y. Shi, P. Li, X. Zhang, X. Feng and W. Hu discussed the results and analyzed the data. G. Zhao supervised the study. K. Zhang, Y. Shi and G. Zhao wrote and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21873068, 21673237, 21573229 and 21422309). G.J.Z. also acknowledges financial support from Open Research Funds of State Key Laboratory of Magnetic Resonance and Atomic and Molecular Physics (Wuhan Institute of Physics and Mathematics of Chinese Academy of Sciences), Double First-Rate and Peiyang Scholar Projects (Tianjin University), the Open Research Funds of State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics (Southeast University), the Frontier Science Project of the Knowledge Innovation Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), and the Project for Excellent Member of CAS Youth Innovation Promotion Association.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112548.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Loh, Q. Bao, G. Eda, M. Chhowalla, Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications. Nat. Chem. 2 (2006) 1015-1024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, Y. Hu, Y. Zhao, G. Shi, L. Deng, Y. Hou, L. Qu, An Electrochemical Avenue to Green-Luminescent Graphene Quantum Dots as Potential Electron-Acceptors for Photovoltaics. Adv. Mater. 23 (2011) 776-780.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Gupta, N. Chaudhary, R. Srivastava, G. Sharma, R. Bhardwaj, S. Chand, Luminscent Graphene Quantum Dots for Organic Photovoltaic Devices. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 133 (2011) 9960-9963.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strongly green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, J. Zhang, C. Qiao, S. Tang, Y. Li, W. Yuan, B. Li, L. Tian, F. Liu, R. Hu, H. Gao, H. Wei, H. Zhang, H. Sun, B. Yang, Strongly Green-Photoluminescent Graphene Quantum Dots for Bioimaging Applications. Chem. Commun. 47 (2011) 6858-6860.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano-graphene oxide for cellular imaging and drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Sun, Z. Liu, K. Welsher, J. Robinson, A. Goodwin, S. Zaric, H. Dai, Nano-Graphene Oxide for Cellular Imaging and Drug Delivery. Nano Res. 1 (2008) 203-212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Pan, J. Zhang, Z. Li, M. Wu, Hydrothermal Route for Cutting Graphene Sheets into Blue-Luminescent Graphene Quantum Dots. Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 734-738.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yang, L. Cao, P. Luo, F. Lu, X. Wang, H. Wang, M. Meziani, Y. Liu, G. Qi, Y. Sun, Carbon Dots for Optical Imaging in Vivo. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131 (2009) 11308-11309.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared excitation/emission and multiphoton-induced fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Li, P. Jing, L. Sun, Y. An, X. Shan, X. Lu, D. Zhou, D. Han, D. Shen, Y. Zhai, S. Qu, R. Zboril, A. Rogach, Near-Infrared Excitation/Emission and Multiphoton-Induced Fluorescence of Carbon Dots. Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1705913.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots for in vivo bioimaging and theranostics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Du, N. Xu, J. Fan, W. Sun, X. Peng, Carbon Dots for In Vivo Bioimaging and Theranostics. Small 15 (2019) 1805087.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A universal strategy for activating the multicolor room‐temperature afterglow of carbon dots in a boric acid matrix

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, W. Zhou, Z. Zhou, H. Zhang, X. Zhang, J. Zhuang, Y. Liu, B. Lei, C. Hu, A Universal Strategy for Activating the Multicolor Room‐Temperature Afterglow of Carbon Dots in a Boric Acid Matrix. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58 (2019) 7278-7283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Q. Meng, L. Wang, J. Zhang, Y. Song, H. Jin, K. Zhang, H. Sun, H. Wang, B. Yang, Highly Photoluminescent Carbon Dots for Multicolor Patterning, Sensors, and Bioimaging. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52 (2013) 3953-3957.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Qu, X. Wang, Q. Lu, X. Liu, L. Wang, A Biocompatible Fluorescent Ink Based on Water-Soluble Luminescent Carbon Nanodots. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51 (2012) 12215-12218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional carbon dots as a therapeutic nanoagent for modulating Cu(II)-mediated β-amyloid aggregation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chung, B. Lee, C. Park, Multifunctional carbon dots as a therapeutic nanoagent for modulating Cu(II)-mediated β-amyloid aggregation. Nanoscale 11 (2019) 6297-6306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-in-matrix boosting intriguing luminescence properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, B. Wang, H. Zhang, J. Yu, Carbon Dots-in-Matrix Boosting Intriguing Luminescence Properties and Applications. Small 15 (2019) 1805504.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Tang, B. Kong, H. Wu, M. Xu, Y. Wang, Y. Wang, D. Zhao, G. Zheng, Carbon Nanodots Featuring Effi cient FRET for Real-Time Monitoring of Drug Delivery and Two-Photon Imaging. Adv. Mater. 25 (2013) 6569-6574.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots produced via space-confined vacuum heating: maintaining efficient luminescence in both dispersed and aggregated states

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhou, P. Jing, Y. Wang, Y. Zhai, D. Li, Y. Xiong, A. Baranov, S. Qu, A. Rogach, Carbon dots produced via space-confined vacuum heating: maintaining efficient luminescence in both dispersed and aggregated states. Nanoscale Horiz. 4 (2019) 388-395.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red‐emitting fluorenes as efficient emitting hosts for non‐doped, organic red-light‐emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Chiang, M. Wu, D. Dai, Y. Wen, J. Wang, C. Chen, Red‐Emitting Fluorenes as Efficient Emitting Hosts for Non‐Doped, Organic Red‐Light‐Emitting Diodes. Adv. Funct. Mater. 15 (2005) 231-238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular design of novel non-planar heteropolycyclic fluorophores with bulky substituents: convenient synthesis and solid-state fluorescence characterization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ooyama, S. Yoshikawa, S. Watanabe, K. Yoshida, Molecular design of novel non-planar heteropolycyclic fluorophores with bulky substituents: convenient synthesis and solid-state fluorescence characterization. Org. Biomol. Chem. 4 (2006) 3406-3409.
J. R. Lakowicz, Principles of fluorescence spectroscopy. Springer Science & Business Media: 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of solid-state carbon dots with intensive emission for white light-emitting devices
